suggestions.name: StringReplacer
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive unit focused exclusively on string replacement operations with various strategies (case sensitivity, limits, character vs string). Extracting them into a StringReplacer class improves separation of concerns and creates a reusable component dedicated to text substitution functionality.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods share a single, focused responsibility: performing string replacement operations. They all take a source string and replace occurrences of specified characters or substrings with new values, with variations for case sensitivity, occurrence limits, and character vs string replacement.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
All methods belong to the "string replacement" domain concept. They represent different strategies for the same core operation - finding and replacing text within strings. This is a cohesive subsystem focused on text substitution with various options (case-sensitive/insensitive, bounded/unbounded, character/string-based).

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates string replacement logic from other string utilities
- Code Reusability: Replacement functionality can be used independently across different contexts
- Complexity Reduction: Removes replacement concerns from the large StringUtils class
- Testability: Replacement logic can be tested in isolation with focused test cases

Step 4: Design Justification
Follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate string replacement concerns. This creates a specialized class with high cohesion (all methods perform text replacement) and low coupling (minimal dependencies). The extraction follows the Single Responsibility Principle by creating a class dedicated solely to string replacement operations.

suggestions.name: ArrayJoiner
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive unit responsible for joining array elements into delimited strings across different primitive types. Extracting them into an ArrayJoiner class improves separation of concerns by isolating array-to-string conversion logic from general string utilities, while the shared capacity calculation method demonstrates strong internal cohesion.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods share a single, well-defined responsibility: joining array elements into delimited strings. All methods follow the same pattern - they take an array of primitive types (byte, char, float, long), a delimiter character, and start/end indices, then produce a string representation with elements separated by the delimiter. The private `capacity` method supports this by calculating optimal StringBuilder capacity.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
These members represent the "array-to-string joining" domain concept. They form a cohesive subsystem that handles the specific task of converting arrays of different primitive types into delimited string representations. The shared `capacity` helper method and consistent algorithm across all join methods demonstrate strong internal cohesion.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates array joining logic from other string utilities
- Code reusability: The ArrayJoiner can be reused across different contexts that need array-to-string conversion
- Complexity reduction: Removes specialized joining logic from the general-purpose StringUtils class
- Testability: Array joining behavior can be tested in isolation with focused test cases
- Performance optimization: The capacity calculation logic is centralized and can be optimized independently

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate a cohesive set of related methods that perform a specific transformation task. The extraction creates a specialized converter class with high cohesion (all methods convert arrays to strings) and low coupling (minimal dependencies beyond StringBuilder and primitive arrays).

suggestions.name: TextNormalizer
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive unit responsible for normalizing text by removing accents and converting special Unicode characters to their ASCII equivalents. Extracting them into a TextNormalizer class improves separation of concerns by isolating complex internationalization logic from general string utilities, making the code more maintainable and reusable.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods share the primary responsibility of text normalization and character conversion. The `stripAccents` method removes accented characters from text, `convertRemainingAccentCharacters` handles specific Unicode characters that standard normalization doesn't cover, and `replaceAll` performs regex-based text replacement. They collectively accomplish the transformation of text by removing or converting special characters to their basic Latin equivalents.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
These members represent the "text normalization" domain concept. They work together to clean and standardize text by removing diacritical marks, converting special Unicode characters to ASCII equivalents, and performing pattern-based replacements. This is a cohesive subsystem focused on character-level text transformations for internationalization and text processing purposes.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates text normalization logic from general string utilities
- Code reusability: Text normalization can be reused across different contexts (search indexing, data cleaning, etc.)
- Complexity reduction: Removes specialized Unicode handling from the general StringUtils class
- Testability: Allows focused testing of normalization edge cases and Unicode character handling

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate specialized text transformation concerns. The cohesive responsibility of normalizing text with complex Unicode character handling justifies extraction into a dedicated class that encapsulates this domain knowledge.

suggestions.name: WhitespaceNormalizer
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive unit focused on cleaning and normalizing whitespace in strings. They share the common responsibility of whitespace manipulation with different strategies for handling nulls and empty strings. Extracting them creates a specialized class that can be reused wherever whitespace normalization is needed.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods share a common responsibility: cleaning and normalizing whitespace in strings. They all deal with removing, trimming, or standardizing whitespace characters - normalizeSpace() collapses multiple whitespaces into single spaces, while the trim methods remove leading/trailing whitespace with different null-handling strategies.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
All methods belong to the "string whitespace normalization" domain. They represent different strategies for cleaning up whitespace in text data, which is a common preprocessing step in text processing, data validation, and user input sanitization.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Improves Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates whitespace cleaning logic from other string utilities
- Enhances Reusability: Whitespace normalization can be used across different contexts (input validation, data processing, formatting)
- Reduces Complexity: Removes specialized whitespace handling from the general StringUtils class
- Better Testability: Whitespace normalization logic can be tested in isolation

Step 4: Design Justification
Follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate whitespace normalization concerns. This creates a cohesive unit with high internal cohesion (all methods manipulate whitespace) and low coupling (minimal dependencies beyond basic string operations).

suggestions.name: StringAbbreviator
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive unit responsible for truncating strings to specified lengths while adding ellipsis or custom markers to indicate truncation. Extracting them into a StringAbbreviator class improves separation of concerns by isolating complex abbreviation algorithms from general string utilities, making the code more maintainable and reusable.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods share a single, well-defined responsibility: shortening strings to fit within specified length constraints while adding visual indicators (ellipsis or custom markers) to show truncation. All four methods are overloaded variants that provide different levels of control over the abbreviation process - from simple max-width truncation to complex offset-based abbreviation with custom markers.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
These methods represent the "string abbreviation" domain concept. They form a cohesive unit focused on text truncation and ellipsis insertion. The methods share common logic for calculating abbreviation boundaries, validating width constraints, and determining optimal truncation points. They work together as a family of related operations that handle different abbreviation scenarios.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates string abbreviation logic from other string utilities
- Code Reusability: Abbreviation functionality can be reused across different contexts (UI components, logging, reports)
- Complexity Reduction: Removes complex abbreviation algorithms from the main StringUtils class
- Testability: Abbreviation logic can be tested in isolation with focused test cases
- Maintainability: Changes to abbreviation behavior are localized to one class

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate a cohesive set of related methods that implement a specific algorithm. The abbreviation methods form a natural unit with high cohesion (all methods truncate strings) and can operate independently with minimal coupling to the parent class.

suggestions.name: BlankStringDetector
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive unit focused on detecting and handling blank strings (empty or whitespace-only content). Extracting them into a BlankStringDetector class improves separation of concerns by isolating whitespace detection logic from general string utilities, creating a reusable component with a single, well-defined responsibility.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods share a primary responsibility of detecting and handling whitespace and blank content in strings. They collectively provide functionality to check if strings are blank (empty or contain only whitespace), find non-blank alternatives, remove whitespace, and perform bulk operations on multiple strings based on their blank status.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
All methods belong to the "blank/whitespace detection and handling" domain. They operate on the concept of "blankness" - distinguishing between strings that have meaningful content versus those that are empty or contain only whitespace characters. This is a cohesive subsystem focused on whitespace-related string analysis and manipulation.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates blank/whitespace detection logic from other string utilities
- Code Reusability: Creates a focused component that can be reused across different contexts where blank detection is needed
- Complexity Reduction: Removes whitespace-specific logic from the broader StringUtils class, making both classes more focused
- Testability: Enables targeted testing of blank detection algorithms and edge cases in isolation

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate concerns based on cohesive functionality. The methods form a cohesive unit around whitespace detection and handling, supporting the Single Responsibility Principle by creating a class dedicated solely to determining and managing blank string states.

suggestions.name: CharSequenceSearcher
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive unit focused on searching, locating, and positioning operations within character sequences. They share common search patterns, use the same INDEX_NOT_FOUND constant, and provide various search strategies (indexOf, lastIndexOf, contains, substring extraction). Extracting them creates a specialized searcher that encapsulates all character sequence positioning logic.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods share a primary responsibility of searching and locating characters, substrings, and patterns within CharSequences. They perform various types of search operations including finding first/last occurrences, counting matches, finding differences between sequences, and extracting substrings based on search criteria. All methods are fundamentally concerned with position-based operations on character sequences.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
All methods belong to the "string searching and positioning" domain. They work with indices, use the common INDEX_NOT_FOUND constant, and provide different search strategies (forward, backward, conditional, pattern-based). They represent a cohesive subsystem for character sequence analysis and manipulation based on positional searches.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates all search/positioning logic from other string utilities
- Code Reusability: Search operations can be reused across different contexts beyond StringUtils
- Complexity Reduction: Removes ~25 search-related methods from the God class, significantly reducing its complexity
- Testability: Search algorithms can be tested in isolation with focused test cases

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate search concerns from general string utilities. The cohesion is high as all methods work with character sequence positioning, and coupling is low as they primarily depend on basic CharSequence operations and shared constants.

suggestions.name: StringPadder
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive unit responsible for string padding and alignment operations. They share common padding logic, constants, and work together to provide left, right, and center padding functionality. Extracting them creates a focused class that encapsulates all string padding concerns while reducing the complexity of the original StringUtils class.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods share a common responsibility: formatting strings by adding padding characters to achieve desired lengths and alignments. They collectively accomplish string padding operations - adding characters to the left (leftPad), right (rightPad), or both sides (center) of strings, along with supporting functionality like repeating characters/strings.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
All methods belong to the "string formatting" domain, specifically focused on padding and alignment operations. They work together to provide comprehensive string padding capabilities, with the repeat() method serving as a core utility for generating padding sequences. The shared constants (SPACE, EMPTY, PAD_LIMIT) are all related to padding operations.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates string padding logic from other string utilities
- Code Reusability: Padding functionality can be reused across different contexts (reports, console output, data formatting)
- Complexity Reduction: Removes specialized formatting logic from the general-purpose StringUtils class
- Testability: Padding logic can be tested independently with focused test cases

Step 4: Design Justification
Follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate formatting concerns. This creates a cohesive unit with high internal cohesion (all methods work toward string padding/alignment) and low coupling (minimal dependencies on external functionality). The extraction follows the Single Responsibility Principle by creating a class dedicated solely to string padding operations.

suggestions.name: StringStripper
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive unit focused on stripping/trimming characters from string boundaries. They share the same core responsibility of removing whitespace or specified characters from the start and end of strings, with various null-handling strategies. Extracting them creates a specialized component that encapsulates all string stripping behavior.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods share a common responsibility: removing whitespace and specified characters from the beginning and end of strings. They collectively accomplish string trimming/stripping operations, handling both single strings and arrays of strings, with various null-safety behaviors (returning empty string, null, or preserving null values).

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
All methods belong to the "string trimming/stripping" domain. They represent different variations of the same core operation - removing unwanted characters from string boundaries. The methods work together as a cohesive unit, with some methods building upon others (e.g., strip() calls stripStart() and stripEnd()).

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Improves Single Responsibility Principle: String stripping logic is isolated from other string utilities
- Enhances Reusability: The stripper can be used independently without importing the entire StringUtils class
- Reduces Complexity: The original StringUtils class becomes more focused by removing this specific concern
- Better Testability: String stripping behavior can be tested in isolation

Step 4: Design Justification
Follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate string trimming concerns from general string utilities. This creates a specialized component with high cohesion (all methods perform string boundary character removal) and low coupling (minimal dependencies on external utilities).

suggestions.name: StringManipulator
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive unit focused on structural string transformations - rotating, splitting, extracting substrings, and truncating. They share the common responsibility of manipulating string structure while preserving content, making them ideal candidates for extraction into a dedicated string manipulation class.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods share the primary responsibility of manipulating strings through structural transformations. They perform operations that change the arrangement, extraction, or segmentation of string content: rotate() shifts character positions, split() methods break strings into arrays, substring() methods extract portions, and truncate() methods shorten strings to specified lengths.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
All methods belong to the "string manipulation" domain, specifically focused on structural operations rather than content analysis or formatting. They represent a cohesive subsystem for transforming strings by changing their structure, length, or arrangement while preserving the original character content.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates string manipulation logic from other string utilities like validation or formatting
- Code Reusability: String manipulation operations can be reused across different contexts without importing the entire StringUtils class
- Complexity Reduction: Reduces the cognitive load of the original StringUtils class by separating structural operations
- Testability: Allows focused testing of string manipulation logic in isolation

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate concerns based on the Single Responsibility Principle. The extracted class represents a cohesive set of string transformation operations that have high internal cohesion (all manipulate string structure) and low coupling with other string utilities.

suggestions.name: StringTruncator
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive unit focused on truncating strings by removing trailing characters, with specialized handling for line endings. Extracting them into a StringTruncator class isolates this specific responsibility and improves the organization of string manipulation utilities.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods share a common responsibility: removing characters from the end of strings, specifically handling line ending characters (CR, LF). The `chomp` method removes line endings while `chop` removes the last character (with special handling for CRLF sequences). Both methods focus on string truncation operations that deal with trailing characters.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
These methods belong to the "string trimming/truncation" domain, specifically focused on removing trailing content. They share the same constants (EMPTY, LF, CR) and both implement logic to handle different line ending scenarios (Unix LF, Windows CRLF). This represents a cohesive subsystem for string tail manipulation.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates string truncation logic from other string utilities
- Code Reusability: String truncation functionality can be reused across different contexts
- Complexity Reduction: Removes specialized truncation logic from the broader StringUtils class
- Testability: Truncation edge cases (empty strings, single characters, different line endings) can be tested in isolation

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate string truncation concerns. The methods have high cohesion (all perform string tail removal) and low coupling (minimal dependencies beyond basic string operations). This creates a focused component following the Single Responsibility Principle.

suggestions.name: StringExtractor
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive unit focused on extracting substrings from specific positions (left and right ends) of input strings. They share common null-handling patterns, edge case validation, and the same underlying responsibility of positional string extraction, making them ideal candidates for extraction into a specialized class.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods share the responsibility of extracting portions of strings from specific positions. The `left()` method extracts characters from the beginning of a string up to a specified length, while `right()` extracts characters from the end of a string up to a specified length. Both handle null inputs and edge cases consistently, returning portions or substrings of the original string.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
These methods belong to the "string extraction" or "substring extraction" domain. They represent positional string operations that extract parts of strings based on length from either end. They share common null-safety patterns, use the same EMPTY constant, and have similar parameter validation logic.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates string extraction logic from other string utilities
- Code Reusability: String extraction operations can be reused across different contexts
- Complexity Reduction: Removes positional string operations from the larger StringUtils class
- Testability: Extraction logic can be tested independently with focused test cases

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate string extraction concerns. The methods exhibit high cohesion as they all perform positional string extraction operations, and they would have low coupling as they only depend on basic string operations and a shared constant.

suggestions.name: StringAnalyzer
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive unit focused on analyzing and decomposing strings through comparison and splitting operations. They share common string examination patterns and work together to provide comprehensive string analysis capabilities. Extracting them creates a focused class with high cohesion around string analysis responsibilities.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods share a primary responsibility of string comparison and string splitting operations. The comparison methods (equals, equalsIgnoreCase, indexOf, indexOfIgnoreCase) handle string matching with case-sensitive and case-insensitive variants, while the split methods (split, splitByWholeSeparator, splitPreserveAllTokens) handle string tokenization with various separator handling strategies.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
All methods belong to the "string analysis and decomposition" domain. They analyze strings to find matches, positions, or break them into constituent parts. The methods work together to provide comprehensive string searching and parsing capabilities, with the comparison methods often serving as building blocks for the splitting operations.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Improves Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates string analysis logic from other string utilities
- Enhances Reusability: String analysis operations can be used independently across different contexts
- Reduces Complexity: Removes complex splitting algorithms from the main utility class
- Improves Testability: String analysis logic can be tested in isolation with focused test cases

Step 4: Design Justification
Follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate string analysis concerns. This creates a cohesive unit where all methods either compare strings or decompose them into parts, representing a single abstraction for string examination and parsing operations.

suggestions.name: StringTransformer
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive unit focused on transforming, converting, and manipulating string data through operations like fuzzy matching, joining, reversing, and encoding conversions. Extracting them into a StringTransformer class separates complex transformation logic from basic string utilities, improving code organization and maintainability.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods share the responsibility of transforming and manipulating string data in various ways. They perform operations like providing default values for null strings, calculating fuzzy string matching scores, joining collections into strings, reversing strings, converting to code points, and converting bytes to strings with charset encoding.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
All methods belong to the "string transformation" domain. They take string or string-related input and produce modified or computed output. Unlike simple validation or formatting, these methods perform more complex transformations, conversions, and computations on string data.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates string transformation logic from other string utilities
- Code Reusability: String transformation operations can be reused across different contexts
- Complexity Reduction: Removes complex transformation algorithms from the main StringUtils class
- Testability: String transformation logic can be tested independently with focused test cases

Step 4: Design Justification
Follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate transformation concerns from basic string utilities. This creates a cohesive unit focused on string manipulation and conversion operations, improving maintainability and allowing for specialized optimization of transformation algorithms.
